entries.definition: noun: the round fruit of a tree of the rose family.
noun: a structure carrying a road or path across a river or gap.
noun: a visible mass of condensed water vapour floating in the atmosphere.
noun: the first appearance of light in the sky before sunrise.
noun: a sound caused by reflection of sound waves from a surface.
noun: a hot glowing body of burning gas produced by combustion.
noun: a small wood or group of trees.
noun: the process or period of gathering crops from fields.
noun: a hard creamy-white material from the tusks of an elephant.
noun: an area of land overgrown with dense tropical vegetation.
noun: a metal container with a lid and spout used for boiling water.
noun: a lamp with a transparent case protecting the flame from the wind.
noun: a field of grass, especially one used for hay or grazing.
adjective: of small width in proportion to length or height.
noun: a very large expanse of sea covering most of the earth's surface.
noun: a person who journeys to a sacred place for religious reasons.
noun: a place from which stone or other materials are extracted.
noun: a question or statement phrased so as to require ingenuity to answer.
noun: a dark area or shape produced by an object blocking light.
noun: wood prepared for use in building and carpentry.
noun: a natural pigment of a dark yellowish-brown colour.
noun: a low area of land between hills or mountains.
noun: a tree or shrub with narrow leaves, typically growing near water.
noun: a heavy inert gaseous element occurring in trace amounts in air.
verb: to have an intense longing or desire for something.
noun: the time at which something is most powerful or successful.

noun: a factual written account of important or historical events in the order of their occurrence.

A chronicle differs from an annals or a simple record in that it attempts to give a continuous, coherent narrative rather than a bare list of dates and facts. The author of a chronicle, sometimes called a chronicler, selects and arranges events so that the reader may follow the unfolding of history across days, years, or centuries. Chronicles have been composed in every literate civilisation: ancient Egyptian scribes recorded the deeds of pharaohs on temple walls; Mesopotamian scribes kept running accounts of royal campaigns and harvests on clay tablets; and medieval European monks filled monastery libraries with accounts of wars, plagues, harvests, and miracles.

The medieval chronicle was a particularly important form of historical writing in Europe between roughly the fifth and fifteenth centuries. Monks and court scribes wrote chronicles in Latin, though vernacular chronicles in French, English, German, and other languages became common from the twelfth century onward. Well-known examples include the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle, which was begun in the reign of King Alfred the Great and continued by successive scribes for several centuries, and the Chronicles of Jean Froissart, which provide a vivid account of the Hundred Years War between England and France.

A chronicle is not the same as a history in the modern sense. A historian analyses cause and effect, weighs evidence, and constructs arguments. A chronicler typically reports what he has seen or heard without attempting a systematic explanation of why events occurred. Despite this limitation, chronicles are invaluable to modern historians because they preserve details, dates, and eyewitness impressions that would otherwise be lost.

In literary use, the word chronicle often refers to any extended narrative account of events over time, whether factual or fictional. The title of C.S. Lewis's series The Chronicles of Narnia uses the word in this looser sense to suggest a sweeping, multi-volume record of an imaginary world's history.
noun: an open space in a forest.
noun: a fierce or angry stare.
noun: a vitreous substance fused onto the surface of pottery.
noun: elegance and beauty of movement or expression.
noun: a particular level of rank, quality, or value.
noun: a berry growing in clusters on a vine, used for wine.
noun: a place of burial for a dead body.
verb: to feed on growing grass or other plants.
adjective: long accepted and recognized as reliable or authoritative.
noun: the questioning of a witness called by the opposing party in a legal proceeding.
adjective: incorporating the newest ideas and most up-to-date features; at the current highest level of development.
noun: the process by which a person controls their own life; or the right of a people to govern themselves.
noun: the act of examining something again, especially a witness after cross-examination.
adjective: occurring over or relating to a long period of time.
adjective: incorporating the latest developments; current or modern.
noun: the mother of one's spouse.
adjective: constantly changing; in a continual state of flux.
adjective: combining or involving several academic disciplines or professional specializations.
noun: the action or process of making important decisions.
adjective: carefully thought out; based on thorough deliberation.
noun: a fresh assessment of something; the act of evaluating again in light of new information.
adjective: referring to items or points stated earlier in the same document or speech.
noun: the actual application of a plan or method, as opposed to theory.
verb: to depend on with full trust or confidence.
noun: the ability to comprehend something; sympathetic awareness or tolerance.
noun: the process of making plans for something; the act of formulating a scheme or program.
noun: facts provided or learned about something or someone.
verb: to keep away from or stop oneself from doing something.
noun: a set of circumstances in which one finds oneself; a state of affairs.
noun: the surroundings or conditions in which a person, animal, or plant lives or operates.
noun: a particular fact, idea, or opinion presented as part of an argument or discussion.
verb: to require something because it is essential or very important.
